note: Added the Lean support, horizon, open-unit simplex, positive baseline, and exact weighted-rank conditions.

problems.detail: def:frontier-elimination-handle: The paper claims this definition characterizes the zero-level set betaStar(delta)=0 via collapsed score equations and eliminated fitted margins, but the Lean declaration only defines the polynomial Phi = M*A - sum D_gt R_g C_t and does not itself deliver the betaStar=0 equivalence or score-equation characterization.

note: Separated the finite-objective rewrite from the separately proved positivity hypotheses and aligned the maximizer and score steps with the verified proof.

In a four-period design with cohorts \(\{2,3,4,\infty\}\), equal cohort shares, no untreated time heterogeneity, and strictly positive proportional effects in every treated cell, the limiting pooled PPML coefficient is negative. → There exists a four-period design with cohorts \(\{2,3,4,\infty\}\), equal cohort shares, no untreated time heterogeneity, and strictly positive proportional effects in every treated cell, for which the limiting pooled PPML coefficient is negative.

RothSantAnnaBilinskiPoe2023: The evidence names Roth, Sant'Anna, Bilinski, and Poe (2023) as a synthesis of the recent DiD literature but does not specifically say it establishes scale-dependent trends or aggregation. — "Nonlinear and semiparametric formulations emphasize that treatment-effect parameters depend on the scale on which trends"

deChaisemartinDHaultfoeuille2020TWFE: The evidence links de Chaisemartin and D'Haultfoeuille (2020) to heterogeneous-treatment TWFE problems and negative weights, but is silent on a positive limiting cohort-shares asymptotic condition. — "\]



Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:cohort-share-limit} is the standard positive limiting cohort shares condition for staggere"

White1982MisspecifiedML: The evidence supports White (1982) for misspecified likelihood and pseudo-likelihood asymptotic foundations, but does not specifically address collinearity with fixed effects in the population metric. — "It rules out collinearity between the treatment indicator and the included fixed effects on the supported collapsed cell"

SantosSilvaTenreyro2006: The evidence says Santos Silva and Tenreyro (2006) justify PPML for multiplicative gravity models with zeros and heteroskedasticity, but it does not specifically establish the extended convention for fixed-effect PPML with separated or all-zero units. — "The extended convention in Definition~\ref{obj:synth_14} is the usual practical device behind fixed-effect PPML with sep"

SantosSilvaTenreyro2011: The evidence supports Santos Silva and Tenreyro (2011) as a PPML reference for multiplicative models with zeros, but is silent on separated or all-zero fixed-effect units as an extended-convention device. — "The extended convention in Definition~\ref{obj:synth_14} is the usual practical device behind fixed-effect PPML with sep"

Wooldridge2023NonlinearDiD: The evidence links Wooldridge (2023) to nonlinear DiD under exponential or Poisson parallel trends, but not specifically to a counterfactual-share proportional treatment-on-the-treated target. — "The same statement also contrasts the pooled coefficient with the counterfactual-share proportional treatment-on-the-tre"

RothSantAnna2023: Roth and Sant'Anna (2023) are described as addressing functional-form sensitivity in DiD, not as studying counterfactual-share proportional treatment-on-the-treated targets. — "The same statement also contrasts the pooled coefficient with the counterfactual-share proportional treatment-on-the-tre"

CallawaySantAnna2021: Callaway and Sant'Anna (2021) support granular group-time effects and aggregations in staggered DiD, but the evidence does not support attributing proportional summaries to that work. — "Granular cohort-time effects and their explicit counterfactual-share aggregations remain the interpretable proportional "

SunAbraham2021: Sun and Abraham (2021) support heterogeneous dynamic event-study effects, but the evidence does not support counterfactual-share proportional summaries. — "Granular cohort-time effects and their explicit counterfactual-share aggregations remain the interpretable proportional "

BorusyakJaravelSpiess2024: Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (2024) support robust event-study or imputation alternatives, but the evidence does not support proportional counterfactual-share summaries. — "Granular cohort-time effects and their explicit counterfactual-share aggregations remain the interpretable proportional "

Andrews1988LLN: Andrews (1988) supplies laws of large numbers for dependent non-identically distributed data, but the evidence does not show that it justifies the Poisson conditional-mean projection interpretation itself. — "Standard pseudo-likelihood references justify treating the criterion as a conditional-mean projection rather than as a f"

In a four-period design with cohorts \(\{2,3,4,\infty\}\), equal cohort shares, no untreated time heterogeneity, and strictly positive proportional effects in every treated cell, the limiting pooled PPML coefficient is negative. → There exists a four-period design with cohorts \(\{2,3,4,\infty\}\), equal cohort shares, no untreated time heterogeneity, and strictly positive proportional effects in every treated cell, for which the limiting pooled PPML coefficient is negative.

SantAnnaZhao2020: The evidence mentions Sant'Anna and Zhao as semiparametric DiD foundations but does not specifically establish that they emphasize scale-dependent trend restrictions and aggregation. — "Nonlinear and semiparametric formulations emphasize that treatment-effect parameters depend on the scale on which trends"

GourierouxMonfortTrognon1984Theory: The evidence supports this work as part of the pseudo-likelihood asymptotic foundation, but is silent on whether the theory paper specifically develops Poisson-type model arguments. — "\citet{White1982MisspecifiedML} frames maximum likelihood estimators under incorrect parametric restrictions as convergi"

AndersonVanWincoop2003: The evidence links Anderson and van Wincoop (2003) to gravity models, but does not specifically establish the full origin, destination, time, and pair fixed-effects claim. — "Gravity applications often pair multiplicative mean models with origin, destination, time, and pair fixed effects \citep"

deChaisemartinDHaultfoeuille2020TWFE: The evidence links de Chaisemartin and D'Haultfoeuille (2020) to heterogeneous-effects problems and negative TWFE weights, but is silent on positive limiting cohort-share asymptotics. — "\]



Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:cohort-share-limit} is the standard positive limiting cohort shares condition for staggere"

White1982MisspecifiedML: White (1982) is described as foundational for misspecified likelihood and pseudo-likelihood asymptotics, but the evidence does not specifically address collinearity with fixed effects in a population pseudo-likelihood metric. — "It rules out collinearity between the treatment indicator and the included fixed effects on the supported collapsed cell"

SantosSilvaTenreyro2006: The evidence supports Santos Silva and Tenreyro (2006) for PPML in multiplicative gravity models with zeros and heteroskedasticity, but not specifically the extended convention for separated or all-zero fixed-effect units. — "The extended convention in Definition~\ref{obj:synth_14} is the usual practical device behind fixed-effect PPML with sep"

RothSantAnna2023: The evidence says Roth and Sant'Anna (2023) clarify functional-form sensitivity in DiD, but it does not specifically establish that they study a counterfactual-share proportional treatment-on-the-treated target. — "The same statement also contrasts the pooled coefficient with the counterfactual-share proportional treatment-on-the-tre"

Andrews1988LLN: The brief lists Andrews (1988) as part of the asymptotic foundation but does not specifically connect it to interpreting Poisson pseudo-likelihood as a conditional-mean projection rather than a full distributional model. — "Standard pseudo-likelihood references justify treating the criterion as a conditional-mean projection rather than as a f"

By contrast, when all treated cells share a common proportional effect, the pooled coefficient recovers that common log multiplier exactly. → By contrast, under the collapsed-rank and untreated-mean conditions, when all treated cells share a common proportional effect, the pooled PPML projection coefficient recovers that common log multiplier exactly.

weaknesses: The Setup section contains six near-identical, machine-generated definitions of the array sampling law and its expectation operator (Definitions synth_18, synth_9, synth_10, synth_4, synth_3, synth_19, synth_1), which are redundant and read as unedited pipeline output; collapse them into a single definition of P_N as an expectation functional (retaining only synth_1, which additionally pins down the mean restrictions) and delete the rest.

Definition synth_3 (Finite-N sampling expectation) has broken LaTeX: literal tab characters appear where arrows should be, e.g. '(\Omega_N	o\mathbb R)	o\mathbb R'; replace with proper \to (this also compounds the redundancy problem above, since the definition would be deleted).

The assumption discussion never confronts the joint strength of Assumptions ass:unit-untreated-exponential-mean and ass:proportional-effects, which together impose a fully parametric multiplicative collapsed mean; add a sentence in Section 3 acknowledging this is a substantive multiplicative-parallel-trends restriction (not weaker than the mean models in Wooldridge2023NonlinearDiD / CallawaySantAnna2021) and note whether it is testable, so the reader can gauge restrictiveness rather than only being told each piece is 'standard'.

The sign-reversal characterization (Theorem thm:primitive-global-frontier) is confined to the frontier {2,3,4,\infty} via Assumption ass:multicohort-frontier-scope, yet the Introduction/Discussion imply a general 'forbidden-comparison' phenomenon; explicitly state in Section 4 that the closed-form Phi characterization and the R_T nonemptiness are established only for this minimal frontier and that extension to general cohort supports C is open, so the generality claim is not oversold.

Definition def:sample-unit-fe-ppml introduces the sample coefficient \hat\beta_N but the paper states it is 'not the object whose sign is characterized' and never connects it to \beta^\star_N via a consistency/sampling argument; either add a short remark stating the population-to-sample bridge is out of scope (and why it is included), or move the sample-coefficient definition to a remark so the reader is not left expecting an asymptotic result that does not appear.

In Section 4 the primitive index Phi is described as a practical 'diagnostic,' but Phi is built from observed collapsed means h_gt that already embed the treated multipliers exp(D_gt \delta_gt); clarify how a practitioner would actually compute Phi (i.e., from estimated collapsed cell means, not from untreated data alone) and reiterate that its negativity diagnoses the projection rather than delivering a corrected causal estimand.

Setup, first page of Section 3: the finite-array sampling-law definitions are duplicated several times and include a visible typo in synth_3, `\Omega_N	o` rendered as `\Omega_N	o`/`o`; consolidate these into one definition and one explanatory paragraph, or move Lean-facing variants to a verification appendix.

Introduction and Theorem 4 discussion: statements such as “For the multicohort frontier studied here” and “the sign of the pooled coefficient is characterized by a primitive index” understate how specialized the result is; revise the prose to explicitly mention the frontier-scope, no-first-period adoption, never-treated support, strict positivity, rank, and untreated exponential-mean conditions whenever presenting the global sign characterization.

Theorem 2 discussion: the sentence “Theorem ... shows that a negative pooled PPML coefficient can occur...” is attached to a theorem statement whose bullets prove region membership and derivative facts but do not directly display `beta^star<0` except through membership; add an explicit bullet `Negative pooled coefficient: beta^star(delta)<0` or make the region-membership bullet transparently include it.

Assumption discussion is too thin on restrictiveness: the paper motivates positivity and collapsed rank, but does not sufficiently discuss how strong the unit untreated exponential mean, deterministic cohort labels, no first-period adopters, never-treated cohort, and proportional-effect homogeneity benchmark are; add a short subsection after the assumptions explaining which are normalization/support conveniences and which are substantive identifying restrictions.

Related Literature: the positioning is generally conservative, but several citations are used as broad placeholders without saying exactly what is borrowed from each, especially nonlinear DiD and proportional PTT references; add one or two sharper sentences distinguishing this paper’s pseudo-true PPML sign result from identification/estimation results in those papers.

Writing/register: the draft reads partly like a Lean export rather than an econometrics working paper, with many `synth_*` labels, long theorem hypotheses, and repeated “we say” definitions interrupting the argument; move machine-verification scaffolding and symbol bookkeeping to an appendix, leaving the main text with econometric primitives, assumptions, and interpretable results.

Definition def:sample-unit-fe-ppml introduces the sample coefficient \hat\beta_N but the paper states it is 'not the object whose sign is characterized' and never connects it to \beta^\star_N via a consistency/sampling argument; either add a short remark stating the population-to-sample bridge is out of scope (and why it is included), or move the sample-coefficient definition to a remark so the reader is not left expecting an asymptotic result that does not appear.

In Section 4 the primitive index Phi is described as a practical 'diagnostic,' but Phi is built from observed collapsed means h_gt that already embed the treated multipliers exp(D_gt \delta_gt); clarify how a practitioner would actually compute Phi (i.e., from estimated collapsed cell means, not from untreated data alone) and reiterate that its negativity diagnoses the projection rather than delivering a corrected causal estimand.

Setup, first page of Section 3: the finite-array sampling-law definitions are duplicated several times and include a visible typo in synth_3, `\Omega_N	o` rendered as `\Omega_N	o`/`o`; consolidate these into one definition and one explanatory paragraph, or move Lean-facing variants to a verification appendix.

Introduction and Theorem 4 discussion: statements such as “For the multicohort frontier studied here” and “the sign of the pooled coefficient is characterized by a primitive index” understate how specialized the result is; revise the prose to explicitly mention the frontier-scope, no-first-period adoption, never-treated support, strict positivity, rank, and untreated exponential-mean conditions whenever presenting the global sign characterization.

Theorem 2 discussion: the sentence “Theorem ... shows that a negative pooled PPML coefficient can occur...” is attached to a theorem statement whose bullets prove region membership and derivative facts but do not directly display `beta^star<0` except through membership; add an explicit bullet `Negative pooled coefficient: beta^star(delta)<0` or make the region-membership bullet transparently include it.

Assumption discussion is too thin on restrictiveness: the paper motivates positivity and collapsed rank, but does not sufficiently discuss how strong the unit untreated exponential mean, deterministic cohort labels, no first-period adopters, never-treated cohort, and proportional-effect homogeneity benchmark are; add a short subsection after the assumptions explaining which are normalization/support conveniences and which are substantive identifying restrictions.

Related Literature: the positioning is generally conservative, but several citations are used as broad placeholders without saying exactly what is borrowed from each, especially nonlinear DiD and proportional PTT references; add one or two sharper sentences distinguishing this paper’s pseudo-true PPML sign result from identification/estimation results in those papers.

Writing/register: the draft reads partly like a Lean export rather than an econometrics working paper, with many `synth_*` labels, long theorem hypotheses, and repeated “we say” definitions interrupting the argument; move machine-verification scaffolding and symbol bookkeeping to an appendix, leaving the main text with econometric primitives, assumptions, and interpretable results.

note: Tightened A_N to admissible extended maximizers and changed the fallback to Lean's existence of a minimum finite-coordinate-norm maximizer, not merely finite beta.
Added the deterministic support assumption and tied finite uniqueness, score collapse, and unit full-rank steps to the exact support/count/rank hypotheses.
Added the strict-positivity hypothesis to the local derivative statement and separated the late-cell residual as its own assembled conclusion.

problems.detail: SantAnnaZhao2020: The brief does not discuss Sant'Anna and Zhao (2020) specifically, so the stated role of that paper is not contradicted but is not established by the supplied evidence. — "Semiparametric and nonlinear extensions clarify how identifying restrictions and target parameters change once the condi"

deChaisemartinDHaultfoeuille2020TWFE: The evidence supports de Chaisemartin and D'Haultfoeuille (2020) for heterogeneous-effects and negative-weight issues in TWFE, but it is silent on a positive limiting cohort-shares or vanishing-mass condition. — "\]



Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:cohort-share-limit} is the positive limiting cohort shares condition: every cohort remains"

White1982MisspecifiedML: The evidence supports White (1982) for misspecified likelihood asymptotics, but it does not establish a full-rank Poisson fixed-effect design condition. — "Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:collapsed-design-rank} is the full-rank Poisson fixed-effect design condition \citep{White1982Mi"

SantosSilvaTenreyro2006: The evidence supports Santos Silva and Tenreyro (2006) for PPML in multiplicative mean models with zeros and heteroskedasticity, but not for high-dimensional PPML computation or the extended handling of separated all-zero unit paths. — "Definition~\ref{obj:def:sample-unit-fe-ppml} fixes notation for the sample PPML coefficient \leanref{sym:hat_beta_N}{\en"

Wooldridge2023NonlinearDiD: Wooldridge (2023) supports nonlinear DiD under exponential/Poisson parallel trends, but the evidence does not support attributing PPML toolkit tasks such as standard errors or finite-sample separation to it. — "Issues such as estimating the granular effects, constructing standard errors, and handling finite-sample separation rema"

NagengastYotov2025: Nagengast and Yotov (2025) is described as connecting multiplicative gravity, PPML, and staggered treatment timing in practice, but the evidence is silent on the specific toolkit tasks named in the sentence. — "Issues such as estimating the granular effects, constructing standard errors, and handling finite-sample separation rema"

SantAnnaZhao2020: The brief does not discuss Sant'Anna and Zhao (2020) specifically, so the stated role of that paper is not contradicted but is not established by the supplied evidence. — "Semiparametric and nonlinear extensions clarify how identifying restrictions and target parameters change once the condi"

deChaisemartinDHaultfoeuille2020TWFE: The evidence supports de Chaisemartin and D'Haultfoeuille (2020) for heterogeneous-effects and negative-weight issues in TWFE, but it is silent on a positive limiting cohort-shares or vanishing-mass condition. — "\]



Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:cohort-share-limit} is the positive limiting cohort shares condition: every cohort remains"

White1982MisspecifiedML: The evidence supports White (1982) for misspecified likelihood asymptotics, but it does not establish a full-rank Poisson fixed-effect design condition. — "Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:collapsed-design-rank} is the full-rank Poisson fixed-effect design condition \citep{White1982Mi"

SantosSilvaTenreyro2006: The evidence supports Santos Silva and Tenreyro (2006) as foundational for PPML with zeros and heteroskedasticity, but is silent on whether it specifically treats standard-error construction or finite-sample separation as separate PPML tasks. — "Issues such as constructing standard errors and handling finite-sample separation remain separate econometric tasks with"

SantosSilvaTenreyro2011: The evidence supports Santos Silva and Tenreyro (2011) as PPML performance evidence, but does not specifically say it covers standard errors or finite-sample separation. — "Issues such as constructing standard errors and handling finite-sample separation remain separate econometric tasks with"

NagengastYotov2025: The evidence places Nagengast and Yotov (2025) in applied staggered gravity settings involving PPML, but does not specifically verify that it addresses standard errors or finite-sample separation. — "Issues such as constructing standard errors and handling finite-sample separation remain separate econometric tasks with"

weaknesses: The abstract and introduction repeatedly assert that beta*(delta) 'is the pseudo-true coefficient of the familiar pooled PPML regression' and 'the coefficient an applied pooled fixed-effect PPML regression is trying to summarize,' but the paper elaborately defines the sample estimator beta_hat_N (Def. 3.6) and then never links it to beta*(delta) by any theorem, explicitly declining to prove plim beta_hat_N = beta*. Fix: in the abstract and Section 1 replace the identity claims with 'the population pseudo-true projection coefficient targeted by pooled PPML' and add one sentence in Section 3 flagging that plim(beta_hat_N)=beta* is a maintained input, not a result, so the diagnostic's empirical relevance rests on that unproven link.

The witness W_4 sets the log multiplier at cell (2,4) to log(4) (a 300% effect) against 1% effects elsewhere, and the prose stresses only that effects are positive, leaving the impression that extreme heterogeneity is required. The Phi formula actually shows reversal at x=1.01 needs only 2y>5x^2+12x-15, i.e. y>~1.11 (an 11% effect). Fix: in Section 4 (after the four-cohort formula) report this threshold to show that modest, empirically plausible heterogeneity (11% vs 1%) already flips the sign, and note that y=4 is chosen only for a clean rational witness.

Assumption restrictiveness is asserted via one-line labels but not honestly discussed. In particular Assumption 3.3 (within-cohort-baseline-limit) imposes deterministic, convergent triangular-array baselines and Assumption 3.2 imposes exponential multiplicative parallel trends, both doing heavy lifting for the collapse, yet neither is examined for plausibility or failure modes. Fix: add a paragraph (end of Section 3 or in Section 5) discussing how restrictive multiplicative parallel trends is relative to additive DiD and what breaks under random/non-convergent baselines.

The multicohort frontier scope (Assumption 3.6: {2,3,4,infinity} subset C, T>=4) is called 'minimal structure' without justification, and Theorem 4.3 bundles the seemingly general equivalence sign(beta*)=sign(Phi) together with this narrow witness-enabling scope, obscuring what is essential. Fix: separate the general Phi <-> sign(beta*) characterization (which appears to need only never-treated-in-C plus rank) from the specific scope used to construct the nonempty witness, stating each with its true minimal hypotheses.

PTT is central to the headline contrast (beta*<0<PTT) but is never given its own definition environment or explicit assumption list; it appears only mid-proof inside Theorem 4.3 with weights omega_gt asserted positive. Fix: promote the counterfactual-share PTT to a numbered Definition in Section 3 alongside beta*, stating its weights and the positivity mechanism, so the two objects being contrasted are introduced symmetrically.

The core contrast 'the pooled coefficient can be negative while PTT stays positive' is restated at near-verbatim length at least six times (abstract, Section 1 twice, Related Literature, Main Results, Discussion twice), inflating length without adding content. Fix: state the contrast once precisely in Section 1, once formally in Section 4, and once interpretively in Section 5, and cut the intermediate restatements in Related Literature and the second Discussion paragraph.

Introduction and Discussion repeatedly describe the large effect as being in a "late cell" or "late-treated cell" even though (2,4) is an early-adopting cohort observed late; replace with "already-treated late calendar cell" or "cohort-2 period-4 cell" throughout.

Section 3 motivates Assumption 5 only as a rank condition but does not explain when it fails in staggered-adoption FE designs; add a short paragraph giving intuitive failures, especially collinearity of treatment with cohort/time effects and the role of never-treated/no-first-period adopters.

The sample PPML coefficient definition is much more detailed than its role in the paper and may distract from the population target; either move it to an appendix or shorten the main-text definition to notation plus a sentence that sample consistency is outside scope.

The primitive sign frontier is presented as using "observed mean components," but those components are population means under the maintained model, not directly observed empirical quantities; qualify this language in the abstract, main theorem discussion, and Discussion to avoid implying an implementable diagnostic without estimation.

The assumption discussion understates the strength of the exponential untreated mean and proportional-effect restrictions; add a paragraph after Assumptions 2-4 explicitly noting these are substantive multiplicative parallel-trends and homogeneous-within-cohort-cell restrictions, not innocuous PPML regularity conditions.

The proof appendix contains several proof-internal Lean references and compressed verification language that may read as code documentation rather than a paper proof; move Lean tags to comments/verification appendix or reduce them to a concise table of verified statements.
